coredata.dc:title: Exploring antimicrobial and biocompatible applications of eco-friendly fluorescent carbon dots derived from fast-food packaging waste transformation
coredata.dc:creator: Thirumalaivasan, Natesan
Mahapatra, Sonalismita
Ramanathan, Ganesan
Kumar, Anuj
Raja, Thandavamoorthy
Muthuramamoorthy, Muthumareeswaran
Pandit, Bidhan
Pandiaraj, Saravanan
Prakash, Santhiyagu
coredata.dc:description: In the face of escalating environmental concerns, particularly the pervasive issue of non-biodegradable fast-food packaging waste, this study introduces a ground-breaking solution that not only addresses waste management but also advances biomedical technology. Utilizing the underexploited resource of Fucoidan, a sulfated polysaccharide from brown algae, we have innovatively transformed fast-food packaging waste into eco-friendly fluorescent carbon dots (FPCDs). These FPCDs were meticulously characterized through advanced techniques like FT-IR, TEM, and XRD, shedding light on their unique structure, morphology, and composition. A significant discovery of this study is the potent antimicrobial properties of these FPCDs, which demonstrate remarkable effectiveness against specific bacterial and fungal strains. This opens new avenues in the realm of biomedical applications, including imaging, drug delivery, and biosensing. Furthermore, extensive toxicity assessments, including the Brine shrimp lethality assay and Adult Artemia toxicity tests, underscore the safety of these nanoparticles, bolstering their applicability in sensitive medical scenarios. Our research presents a compelling dual approach, ingeniously tackling environmental sustainability issues by repurposing waste while simultaneously creating valuable materials for biomedical use. This dual benefit underscores the transformative potential of our approach, setting a precedent in both waste management and medical innovation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-use plastics
Food packaging waste
Carbon dots
Antibacterial properties
Biocompatibility
Hemolysis
Brine shrimp lethality assay
Fucoidan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Minimum inhibitory (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education – Kingdom of Saudi Arabi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research and Innovation, “Ministry of Education” in Saudi Arabia for funding this research ( IFKSUOR3-301-3 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Depicted in the high-resolution TEM image are the FPCDs(b) The size distribution of the FPCDs is visualized in the histogram.

(a and b) Scanning electron microscopy images were used to visualize the synthesized FPCDs, scale bar 0.5 μm (c, d, and e) Elemental mapping analysis was employed to gain insights into the distinct distribution of carbon (green), oxygen (purple), and nitrogen (red) within the FPCDs. f) overlayed image of (c–e) images, scale bar 10 μm.
The characterization of FPCDs involved: a) Analysis of X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, and b) Analysis of FT-IR spectra.

(a) Absorption and (b) fluorescence emission spectrum of the FPCDs when dissolved in water. The small images show the FPCDs under (a) Day light and (b) illumination with 365 nm UV light, respectively.
Antimicrobial activity and MIC and MBC value on synthesized FPCD sagainst chosen pathogenic strains.
Antibiofilm properties on synthesized FPCD sagainst chosen pathogenic strains.
Inhibitory effects of biofilm formation on synthesized FPCDs against chosen pathogenic bacterial strains (A) and Candida strains (B) through Crystal violet assay.
Toxicity assessment on synthesized FPCDs using adult male and female Artemia sp..
Confocal Fluorescent visuals of 3T3 cells treated with 200 μg/ml of FPCDs show: (a) DIC view, (b) blue light emission, and (c) a merged image, all under a blue excitation wavelength of 420 nm.

Schematic diagram illustrating the hydrothermal synthesis method is employed to create Fluorescent Carbon Dots Derived from Fast-Food Packaging Waste (FPCDs). These FPCDs are modified with fucoidan, making them suitable for applications in antimicrobial and biomarker applications.
LC50 and Ic50 value on synthesized FPCDs against human blood and Artemia sp..

head.ce:title: Exploring antimicrobial and biocompatible applications of eco-friendly fluorescent carbon dots derived from fast-food packaging waste transformation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the face of escalating environmental concerns, particularly the pervasive issue of non-biodegradable fast-food packaging waste, this study introduces a ground-breaking solution that not only addresses waste management but also advances biomedical technology. Utilizing the underexploited resource of Fucoidan, a sulfated polysaccharide from brown algae, we have innovatively transformed fast-food packaging waste into eco-friendly fluorescent carbon dots (FPCDs). These FPCDs were meticulously characterized through advanced techniques like FT-IR, TEM, and XRD, shedding light on their unique structure, morphology, and composition. A significant discovery of this study is the potent antimicrobial properties of these FPCDs, which demonstrate remarkable effectiveness against specific bacterial and fungal strains. This opens new avenues in the realm of biomedical applications, including imaging, drug delivery, and biosensing. Furthermore, extensive toxicity assessments, including the Brine shrimp lethality assay and Adult Artemia toxicity tests, underscore the safety of these nanoparticles, bolstering their applicability in sensitive medical scenarios. Our research presents a compelling dual approach, ingeniously tackling environmental sustainability issues by repurposing waste while simultaneously creating valuable materials for biomedical use. This dual benefit underscores the transformative potential of our approach, setting a precedent in both waste management and medical innovation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization and globalization have intensified environmental challenges, particularly the rise in plastic waste (Marshall and Farahbakhsh, 2013; Shen et al., 2020). Single-use plastics, commonly found in fast-food packaging, are convenient but environmentally detrimental (Chen et al., 2021). Their use grows during health crises, exacerbating pollution. These plastics release toxins when degrading, threatening both the environment and health. A significant portion of this waste lands in oceans and landfills, raising waste management issues (Rajmohan et al., 2019). Carbon dots, tiny bioluminescent nanomaterials, can be produced via methods like thermal decomposition. This research examined these dots for their characteristics, safety, and antimicrobial qualities, with tests confirming their biological safety (Rimal et al., 2020). They have notable healthcare potential due to their antimicrobial nature. Converting fast-food plastic waste into carbon dots presents an eco-friendly solution. Adding fucoidan during production could enhance their functions, underscoring nanotechnology's potential and the adaptability of carbon dots (Lam et al., 2019).

The increasing environmental strain from non-biodegradable fast-food packaging necessitates innovative waste management strategies (Ahmed et al., 2018). As a significant contributor to global waste, the fast-food industry's predominantly plastic packaging demands effective recycling solutions (Albert and Olutayo, 2021; Davenport Huyer et al., 2020). Integrating biodegradable substances, such as Fucoidan from brown algae, presents an eco-friendly alternative. As the quest for new biomaterials broadens, so does the imperative to address plastic waste, which persists for decades. Techniques like thermal recycling harness energy from these plastics, and research has shown that they can be a source of valuable carbon nanotubes (Altalhi et al., 2013; Zhuo and Levendis, 2014). The burgeoning interest in sustainable waste management seeks to not only reduce environmental harm but also to transform waste into valuable resources (Agustia et al., 2019). The concept of converting fast-food packaging, typically an environmental burden, into beneficial materials stands as a promising avenue in sustainability (Li et al., 2020; Specht et al., 2014). While traditional recycling has primarily focused on turning waste back into plastic products, the challenges of single-use plastics underscore the urgency for inventive recycling methods that add value to repurposed materials.

Fast-food packaging waste into eco-friendly fluorescent carbon dots (FPCDs) are nano-sized materials that have gained significant attention due to their unique properties and potential in biomedical applications, such as antimicrobial agents and drug delivery systems. An innovative approach has emerged to produce FPCDs from waste materials, offering both waste reduction and the creation of valuable materials. These particles have piqued interest in sectors like biomedicine and electronics. Fucoidan, a sulfated polysaccharide from brown seaweeds, is renowned for its therapeutic benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antitumor activities. Its integration enhances the bioactivity and biocompatibility of FPCDs. By merging FPCDs with fucoidan, there's potential to revolutionize sustainable recycling and widen product applications. To foster sustainability, the goal is to repurpose fast-food packaging waste, pairing it with fucoidan to create eco-friendly FPCDs. These FPCDs combine luminescence with biocompatibility, making them suitable for bioimaging and sensors. Given the global increase in plastic use, as noted by (Ahuja et al., 2023), and the emphasis by entities like the USA and EU on recycling, employing nanotechnology, as suggested by (Yadav et al., 2021; Balakrishnan and Pattathil, 2018)), could transform plastics into versatile FPCDs.

This study investigates the creation of carbon dots using food packaging waste (FPCDs), improved with fucoidan for enhanced functionality and safety. The distinct shape and antimicrobial properties of FPCDs, along with their varied applications in healthcare and imaging as indicated by Cohen et al. (2020) and Falina et al. (2022), are noteworthy. The research underscores their contribution to environmentally friendly recycling methods, tackling plastic waste issues, and combining sustainable practices with cutting-edge material development. The fabrication process of these dots, which includes thermal decomposition and fucoidan, aims at optimizing their medical use and safety, as evidenced by Brine shrimp lethality and Adult Artemia toxicity evaluations. These advancements have been shown to boost the performance of FPCDs and decrease their toxicity, underscoring their viability in biomedical contexts. The synthesis technique, involving thermal decomposition and fucoidan, is centered on assessing their medical applicability and in vitro toxicity. This method not only presents an innovative solution to recycle single-use plastics but also expands the scope of biomedical research and the development of new materials.

In the research, food packaging items like plastic plates and bowls were collected from food courts in Chennai, Tamilnadu, India. Deionized water served as the dispersing solvent during sample preparation. For antimicrobial and Artemia toxicity evaluations, 99% pure ethanol was obtained from Himedia, India. Additionally, the antimicrobial tests employed Muller Hinton agar and nutrient broth, both sourced from Himedia and verified to be 99% pure.

This research aimed to produce fluorescent carbon dots from disposable plastics sourced from Chennai. The plastics, primarily from food packaging plates and bowls, were first meticulously cleaned to eliminate residues and contaminants. After ensuring they were free of foreign matter, these plastics were manually segmented into minute fragments and placed in a ceramic dish. Using a muffle furnace, these fragments were subjected to a gradual temperature increase from ambient levels to 400 °C over a span of 2 h, elevating at 20 °C every minute. The furnace's temperature was then methodically reduced back to room temperature, descending at 10 °C every minute. The resultant powders, which turned black, cooled naturally. These cooled powders were then ground into a fine consistency.

To this powdered form, 10 ml of a 3% H2O2 solution was introduced. This combination was agitated at room temperature for 2 h using a magnetic mixer. The mixed solution was then shifted to a 100 ml Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave, undergoing a hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 5 h. Once the autoclave naturally returned to ambient temperature, the resulting mixture was passed through a 0.2 μm Whatman filter paper to separate minute residues. The ensuing dark-brown liquid (referred to as FPCDs) was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for half an hour, producing a transparent solution. This solution was further refined by dialysis, utilizing a 1 kDa MWCO membrane, over three days. To solidify the dispersed FPCDs, techniques such as ultracentrifugation at 15,000 rpm or freeze-drying were applied.

Carbon dots were synthesized from food packaging plastic plates. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted in a 50 ml Teflon-lined autoclave from Baoshishan, India. After the materials were cleaned and freeze-dried, their properties were examined using an FT-IR spectrometer in standard atmospheric conditions, scanning between wave numbers of 4000 cm−1 and 400 cm−1. The UV–visible spectrum of the carbon dots, when dissolved in ultrapure water, was observed using a V-630 Bio spectrometer by Jasco, a company based in Japan. These measurements were taken at ambient temperature in a quartz cell with a 1 cm optical path, capturing absorption spectra between wavelengths of 200–800 nm. To gauge the size and crystalline nature of the carbon dots, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used. The size and form of these dots were thoroughly evaluated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Jeol JSM IT 800). The elemental makeup of the dots was determined using an EDX tool, and an elemental analyzer from Oxford Instruments measured the quantities of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) within them.

The test pathogenic bacterial strains, with 2 g positive strains, namely, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 2642) and Salmonella typhi (MTCC 733), and 2 g negative strains, Streptococcus pneumoniae (MTCC, 1936) and Enterococcus faecalis (MTCC 439). Additionally, two Candida strains, C. tropicalis (MTCC 184) and C. galbrata (MTCC 3984), were chosen for antimicrobial and antibiofilm inhibitory research.

The bactericidal capabilities of FPCDs were assessed using the agar well diffusion method. Using a procedure adapted from (Prakash, 2023), overnight bacterial cultures with concentrations of 1 × 108 cells ml−1 were applied onto Muller Hinton Agar (MHA) dishes. Wells in the agar received 100 μl of the formulated FPCDs at a concentration of 100 μg/ml. For comparison, Erythromycin (20 μg/ml) was used as a positive control, while distilled water (100 μl) served as a negative control on the same plate. Post a 24-h incubation period at 37 °C, the resultant zones of bacterial and candida growth inhibition were quantified by measuring the diameter around each well. All tests were performed three times for consistency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum inhibitory (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)
ce:section.ce:para: A micro-dilution method with 96-well plates was used to determine the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of FPCDs at concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 μg/ml. After loading and drying the CDs and added to 100 μl of bacterial strains (1 × 108 CFU ml−1) and Candida strains (1 × 106 CFU ml−1) to the 96- micro well plates, individually. Incubation occurred at 37 °C for 24 h (bacteria) and 48 h (candida). After incubation, 0.4% resazurin stain was added, and MIC was determined as the lowest concentration of FPCDs with no color change from purple to pink. For Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC), followed method described by (Prakash, 2023). A loopful of culture from each MIC test was loop full culture inoculated on Nutrient Agar (NA) plates, and the lowest concentration with no pathogen growth was considered the MBC.

After evaluating the antibacterial effectiveness, the anibiofilm inhibiton was assessed by the exploring FPCDs against to the most susceptible bacterial and Candida pathogenic strians, as described by (Durgadevi et al., 2019). Each test bacterial pathogen (1 × 108 CFU ml) and Candida strians (1 × 106 CFU ml) into 96-well plates containing 200 μl of Trypticase soy yeast broth (TSYB) with increasing concentrations of carbon dots solutions (ranging from 10 to 100 μg/ml) and incubated them at 37 °C for 24 h for bacterial strians and 48 h for candida strians. Additionally, the negative control consisting of only TSYB (200 μl of culture) was maintained in same micro well plates. After the incubation periods, broth culture contain planktonic cells from both control and extract incorporated wells were removed the and ashed with twice time using sterile distilled water and then tried in room temprature for the quantified biofilm inhibition (%) through 0.1% crystal violet staining using an ELISA reader.

To evaluate the effectiveness of FPCDs against biofilm development, chosen test pathogenic microbial strains were introduced to sterilized 1 × 1 cm glass slides in the presence of 40, 60, and 80 μg/ml concentrations, as well as in their absence. After a 24 h-48 h incubation at 37 °C, the slides were rinsed with sterilized water and subsequently stained with a 0.4% solution of crystal violet for observation under a light microscope (Nikon [Eclipse 80i], Pvt. Ltd, India) adapting the protocol established by (Nangan et al., 2023).

The ability of FPCDs sourced from food-container plastics to cause hemolysis was analyzed via a microplate technique adopted by (Hoque et al., 2017; Ramasubburayan et al., 2017). Human blood samples, obtained from a healthy donor, and were retrieved and stored in vacuum container with a 2.7% EDTA solution to prevent clotting. These erythrocytes were mixed in 10% PBS and further diluted to a 1:10 ratio. A 100 μl aliquot from this dilution was dispensed into 96-well microplates. Sequentially, 0.1 mL of various concentrations ranged from 12.5 to 150 μg/ml of the potent carbon dots were incorporated in 96 micro well plate. Concurrently, control samples, one with just PBS (Negative) and another with 0.1% Triton X-100 (positive), were added. The configuration was sustained at 37 °C for 60 min, followed by a centrifuge phase at 2000 rpm for a decade of minutes. The resulting supernatant's optical density was captured at 540 nm via an ELISA instrument. Hemolysis percentage was ascertained using the criteria of ASTM standard practice F 756–00.

The toxicity of the FPCDs was evaluated using the brine shrimp Artemia sp., as a reference species. Briefly, different dilutions (ranging from 6.25 to 150 μg/ml) of the FPCDs were set up in 24-well plates. To each well, 1000 μl of purified seawater holding ten live Artemia nauplii was added. Pure seawater served as the baseline control. This setup was kept at 25 °C for 24 h. At the end of this incubation duration, the number of surviving nauplii in both the test and control samples was recorded, and the percentage of mortality rate was calculated. The median lethal concentration (LC50) was calculated using a statistical probit method using SPSS.26 version.

The toxicological evaluation of the created FPCDs was performed on both male and female adult Artemia sp., individually, adapting the procedure outlined by (Karaman et al., 2023). Concisely, each glass Petri dish was filled with 20 ml of purified seawater and then exposed to different concentrations from 100 to 225 μg/ml. Ten specimens each of both male and female Artemia were separately introduced into the dishes and maintained at a consistent temperature of 25 ± 1 °C at 24 and 72 h. During the incubation periods, the survival rate of the Artemia was noted. Subsequent calculations of mortality rate, LC50 were executed via probit analysis. Additionally, any morphological changes in the exposed Artemia across each concentration were meticulously inspected under a 10 × magnification using an inverted microscope, integrated with a Nikon Elips Model software-aided camera.

Toxicity assessed in the synthesized FPCDs on HeLa cells using the MTT assay following methods described by (Ramasubburayan et al., 2023). HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. After replacing the culture media with one containing different FPCDs concentrations (25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 500 μg/ml) and incubating for another 48 h, cell viability was determined using MTT stain (1 mg/ml). Each well was treated with a combination of media and MTT, incubated for 4 h, and then treated with DMSO to dissolve formazan crystals from live cells. After the incubation periods, absorbance at 570 nm was read using an ELISA reader, and cell viability was computed using specific formulas.
(1)
%
of
Cell
Viability = Mean
absorbance
(
treated
samples
untreated
samples
)
× 100

Data acquired from this study were subjected to Mean ± SD and further analyzed using ANOVA test with a post hoc multiple comparison of SNK test at a significant level of 5% using SPSS 16.0. Probit analysis was performed to check out the toxicity of extract by EPA probit program (1.5, Cincinnati, USA).

A pioneering approach was conceived, merging air thermal oxidation with hydrothermal methodologies. The primary objective was to enhance the treatment of plastic waste commonly found in food packaging. Our initial experiments saw these plastics being treated in an open-air environment, heated to a temperature of 500 °C, sustained for a duration of 2 h. Observations post this thermal treatment indicated a prominent change in the material properties. Notably, the plastic underwent cross-linking, a process which led to its transformation in color, transitioning from its original hue to a distinct dark brown. A deeper analysis of this thermally treated plastic revealed the emergence of a range of chromophoric systems. Furthermore, there was a significant presence of oxygen-rich functional groups within the modified material. Such changes are indicative of the plastic's elevated reactivity, showcasing a stark contrast when juxtaposed against its original form. Building upon the foundation laid by the initial oxidative process, the next phase of our research involved subjecting the oxidized plastic to a hydrothermal procedure. The process was carried out under controlled conditions at a temperature of 200 °C, incorporating the use of a fucoidan compound. Through the intricate combination of these procedures, we successfully observed the formation of FPCDs (carbon dots derived from food packaging waste). In summary, our comprehensive approach, which blends thermal oxidation and hydrothermal methods, represents an innovative step forward in addressing and converting plastic waste from food packaging (as depicted in Scheme 1 ). These resulting FPCDs hold potential for various applications, and their full scope will be further explored in future research endeavors.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were employed to investigate the morphology and size distribution of the FPCDs. SEM images revealed that the FPCDs predominantly exhibited a spherical shape with a relatively uniform size distribution. The average size of the FPCDswas determined to be approximately 6 ± 3 nm, corroborating their nanoscale dimensions as observed in the UV–vis spectra. TEM images further confirmed the spherical morphology and provided detailed insights into the surface features and nanostructure of the FPCDs. The HR-TEM images were used to examine the analyzed FPCDs. The images reveal the FPCDs as clusters of almost spherical particles. Their amorphous attributes are outlined in the HR-TEM analysis in Fig. 1 a. Additional analysis of the FPCDs in a water solution indicated an average size ranging from 3 to 7 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. SEM images of FPCDs displayed a consistent, spherical nanoform, affirming their identity as carbon dots as seen in Fig. 2 a and b. The elemental analysis spotlighted the spread of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N). Carbon was illustrated in green, signifying its even spread, while oxygen was marked in purple, suggesting a lesser concentration than carbon. In contrast, intermittent red spots signified the even scarcer presence of nitrogen. Fig. 2c–f depict a composite image capturing the intricate elemental distribution. The prevailing presence of carbon corroborates the FPCDs designation, with the traces of oxygen and nitrogen possibly pointing to functional groupings or doping. In summary, both SEM and elemental scrutiny offer a thorough insight into the structure and make-up of FPCDs, paving the way to delve into their multifaceted uses. XRD analysis, demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, emphasizes a notable broad peak at 2θ = 200, highlighting the existence of a graphitic carbon structure. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 3b reveals specific absorption bands, signifying varied vibrations, including O–H stretching at 3440 cm−1, C–H stretching at 2925 cm−1, CO stretching at 1712 cm−1, and C–O–C bending at 1409 cm−1. EDX analysis, depicted in Fig. 3b, determines the primary constituents of the FPCDs as carbon (58.8%) and oxygen (41.2%), strongly suggesting an oxygen-rich carbon structure. In summary, the accumulated data definitively categorizes the materials as FPCDs, exhibiting a graphite-like structure with a significant presence of oxygen groups on their surfaces. These functional groups are known to play a crucial role in the antimicrobial and biofilm inhibitory properties of the FPCDs.

The main aim of this study was to delve into the optical traits of the produced FPCDs. When combined with water, these FPCDs formed a solution that was clear and radiated a vivid blue luminescence under UV light exposure. Fig. 4 a illustrates the UV–visible absorption profiles, which highlight a significant band near 350 nm. This is indicative of the n-π* transition linked to the CO configurations. Importantly, there was a notable fluorescence peak at 528 nm (marked by a red line) in the water-based solution when illuminated at 320 nm. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of the FPCDs, as shown in Fig. 4b, changed with the variation in excitation wavelengths. A peak PL emission was recorded at 528 nm when illuminated at 440 nm. In summary, the FPCDs exhibit remarkable optical attributes. Their ability to emit a bright blue light in water, along with their shifting emission responses based on the excitation wavelength, underscores their versatility, setting the stage for further exploration and harnessing the potential of FPCDs across various sectors.

FPCDs are extensively used in areas like drug delivery, bioimaging, optoelectronics, energy conservation, photocatalysis, and more. Transforming non-recyclable plastic waste into such valuable resources is a significant step towards environmental conservation. The synthesized FPCDs demonstrated notably strong antibacterial and antifungal properties, as evidenced by their zones of inhibition, MIC, and MBC values against the test pathogens. Of the four pathogenic strains, E. faecalis and P. aeruginosa showed the largest zones of inhibition (23 ± 1.25 and 21 ± 0.47 mm, respectively), while S. typhi displayed a minimum inhibitory level of 18 ± 0.82 mm. Among the Candida species tested, C. galbrata exhibited a significant inhibitory level of 24 ± 0.47 mm, surpassing C. tropicalis. Further evaluation of the bacteriostatic (MIC), bactericidal (MBC), and fungicidal (MFC) properties revealed that the synthesized FPCDs effectively halted the growth of all pathogens, showcasing lower MIC (20–80 μg/ml), MBC (40–100 μg/ml), and MFC (40–100 μg/ml) values (Fig. 5 ). Among the strains tested, E. faecalis was the most sensitive to the FPCDs at a concentration of 20 μg/ml. Similarly, at the same concentration, C. galbrata was the only Candida species sensitive to the FPCDs. This suggests that the FPCDs likely contain significant bioactive compounds that could pave the way for new antimicrobial therapies due to interact with bacterial and fungal cellular components, disrupting vital processes necessary for their growth and survival. FPCDsmay penetrate the cellular membranes, causing structural and functional damage. Their broad-spectrum activity against different strains suggests multiple modes of interaction or multiple target sites within the microbial cells.

Bacterial pathogens forming biofilms present a major challenge in hospitals, contributing to the rise of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). This in turn has resulted in increased morbidity, mortality, and medical costs. Against this backdrop, leveraging nanomaterial-derived metabolites to disrupt biofilm formation offers a promising therapeutic alternative to counteract biofilm-related infections. In this study, we assessed the biofilm inhibition capability of the FPCDs against certain pathogens, including P. aeruginosa, S. typhi, S. pneumonia, E. faecalis, C. tropicalis, and C. galbrata. The testing was done at sub-lethal concentrations (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/ml) using the crystal violet (CV) assay in multi-well plates. At a concentration of 40 μg/ml, the FPCDs inhibited biofilm formation by 22–50%. However, a notable reduction of over 90% was observed at 100 μg/ml for several pathogens (Figs. 6 and 7
). This concentration, 100 μg/ml, was thus identified as the Minimal Biofilm Inhibitory Concentration (MBIC). Interestingly, the biofilm inhibitory efficacy of FPCDs in our study was somewhat less potent than previous findings reported by (Muthuramalingam et al., 2022).

The observed hemolysis percentage increased with the concentration of the FPCDs. Among the CDs, hemolysis rates varied between 13% and 40% at concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 μg/ml (Fig. S1). In a related study by (Ouattar et al., 2022; Prakash et al., 2013), the anti-hemolysis activity of carbon dots derived from traditional Chinese medicinal plants was evaluated. They noted the highest hemolysis at 10 mg/ml, with decreased hemolysis at lower FPCDs concentrations. Also, the IC50 value is 68.638 μg/ml was calculated (Table 1 ). These findings suggest that the synthesized carbon dots did not significantly disrupt the erythrocyte cell membrane, indicating their low toxicity. Typically, non-toxic nanoparticles or nanomaterials are preferred for advanced research in pharmaceuticals, with the goal of developing potential drug candidates to treat various diseases.

Artemia spp. are increasingly being recognized as suitable biological models for nanoecotoxicity testing. Recently, the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) introduced guidelines to evaluate the acute aquatic toxicity of nanomaterials using standardized testing procedures with Artemia spp. nauplii (Babaei et al., 2022; Moovendhan et al., 2014). In the present study has been attempted to made by the brine shrimpslethal toxicity on different concentrations of FPCDs. From this, the mortality percentage as noted at end the incubation periods at 24 h. After the incubation periods significantly (P < 0.05) mortality percentage calculated, and it ranged from 7 to 97% respectively and the maximum mortality 83 and 97% as recorded at 100 and 200 μg/ml concentration of FPCDs. At the same time behind the test concentration from 75 to 6.25 μg/ml was registered the least mortality percentage ranged from 53 to 7% over the 100 and 200 μg/ml concentrations (Fig. S2). Whereas, the LC50 value of the Artemia cytotoxicity properties as predicted in 65.435 μg/ml (Table 1). Confirmative with present study, (Avram et al., 2023) performed carried an elaborate study to evaluate the ecotoxicological effects of nanomaterials in aquatic environment using Artemia spp. as a model organism. These studies encompass a range of materials including silver, titanium dioxide, and different carbon derivatives like fullerene, carbon black, carbon nanodots, and graphene. (Hartl and Humpf, 2000) stated that the survival rate of Artemia spp. when exposed to carbon-based nanomaterials is influenced not just by their developmental stage but also by the specific nanomaterial, its production process, and it's functionalization.

Research in nanotoxicology spans multiple domains, including biology, pathology, and notably, pharmacology, emphasizing the application of nanomaterials and nanodevices for diagnostic and therapeutic goals. A significant challenge for toxicologists is identifying both in vitro and in vivo tests that faithfully represent the toxicity potential of nanoparticles in humans and the environment. Standardizing tests for in vitro and in vivo analysis is essential to foster the creation of swifter and more effective screening methods and enhance our predictive capabilities for toxicity (Kumar et al., 2023).

Generally, carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit mild toxicity to the majority of aquatic life forms, with their median effective concentration (LC50) ranging from 10 to 100 μg/ml. Notably, in the present LC50 values for male and female Artemia animals are 71.374 and 48.625 μg/ml, respectively. These values are considerably lower than many toxicity benchmarks, providing strong evidence that the synthesized FPCDs we produced have minimal/non-toxic effects. The toxicity of these nanomaterials is influenced by various factors. The inherent nature of the material, defined by its physicochemical properties and manufacturing methods, can alter its toxicity. Factors like size, functional groups, and the choice of solvent for preparation are among the determinants. Sensitivity to these materials varies among species; algae are the most vulnerable, followed by crustaceans, fish, and bacteria. The duration of exposure also plays a critical role in determining the toxic effects of carbon-based nanomaterials (Pikula et al., 2023).

The assessment of FPCDs' cytotoxicity involved conducting an MTT assay with 3T3 cells. As shown in Fig. S3, the results indicate that even at a high concentration of 1 mg/ml, cellular viability remains at 89.4%. This suggests that FPCDs exhibit minimal toxicity and are highly biocompatible. FPCDs showcase strong blue fluorescence, hinting at their use as effective fluorescent markers. The distinct properties of FPCDs make them suitable for cellular labeling, even without exhaustive data on their application methods. FPCDs present a fresh perspective in 3T3 cells imaging, offering an alternative to traditional dyes plagued by issues like photobleaching and limited longevity (Fig. 10 ). Their use minimizes the risk of disturbing 3T3 cells functions, essential for genuine cellular behavior studies. Further research is needed to assess FPCD's long-term stability, labeling efficacy across different organisms, and their role in drug delivery. Challenges include ensuring consistent labeling and validating their safety across different exposure levels. In essence, FPCDs hold promise in 3T3cellular labeling due to their fluorescence and biocompatibility, marking their significance in biomedical research and treatments.

In conclusion, this study lays a robust foundation for the utilization of FPCDs in a range of fields, especially in biomedicine. The carbon dots developed through this research are characterized by their antimicrobial qualities and low toxicity levels, making them suitable for various applications. The research highlights the transformation of plastic waste into beneficial materials, simultaneously addressing environmental and health challenges. There is a need for further research to refine the synthesis process of FPCDs and explore their broader biomedical applications. Assessing the long-term environmental effects of producing FPCDs is also crucial. The current reliance on single-use plastics, particularly highlighted during health crises, underscores the significance of converting waste into useful products through recycling. This study focuses on converting fast-food packaging waste into environmentally friendly FPCDs. Extensive analysis has verified their spherical structure, with an average size of 6 ± 3 nm, distinct chemical compositions, pronounced antimicrobial properties, and compatibility with biological systems. The incorporation of fucoidan materials further reduced their toxicity. Ultimately, this research introduces a viable method for recycling food packaging waste and paves the way for new carbon-based materials in the biomedical field.

Natesan Thirumalaivasan: Investigation, Methodology. Sonalismita Mahapatra: Data curation. Ganesan Ramanathan: Software. Anuj Kumar: Formal analysis. Thandavamoorthy Raja: Software. Muthumareeswaran Muthuramamoorthy: Visualization. Bidhan Pandit: Validation, Shalini Verma, Validation. Saravanan Pandiaraj: Formal analysis. Santhiyagu Prakash: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research and Innovation, “Ministry of Education” in Saudi Arabia for funding this research (IFKSUOR3-301-3).
